title: Where Skills Run, and Packaging as Plugins
objective: Map the surfaces skills run on and their runtime constraints, the Claude Code extensions to the standard, and how to package and distribute skills as plugins via marketplaces.

sources.title: Agent Skills — Overview (Claude Platform Docs)
sources.trust_rationale: Anthropic's canonical product documentation for Agent Skills.

sources.title: Extend Claude with skills (Claude Code Docs)

beats.narration: The same skill format runs across three surfaces, but how you install it differs. On claude.ai you upload custom skills as a zip through Settings and Features, and they're personal to you. In Claude Code, skills are just filesystem directories — no upload at all. On the Claude API, you reference a skill_id in the container parameter, and custom skills are uploaded through the skills endpoints and shared across the whole workspace.

visual.alt: Three surface cards: claude.ai, Claude Code, and the Claude API with their upload models

beats.narration: Crucially, the runtime powers differ too, and this catches people out. On the Claude API a skill has no network access and cannot install packages at run time — only pre-installed ones work. On claude.ai, network access varies with user and admin settings. In Claude Code, a skill has full network access, like any program on your computer, though you're advised to install packages locally rather than globally.

visual.alt: Runtime constraints per surface: claude.ai varies, API has no network, Claude Code full network

beats.narration_intro: A constraint you must design around.
beats.prompt: On which surface can a skill NOT access the network or install packages at runtime?
beats.correct_feedback: Correct. On the Claude API there's no network access and no runtime package installation.
beats.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. It's the Claude API: there a skill has no network access and cannot install packages at runtime.

beats.narration: Claude Code skills follow the Agent Skills open standard, then add extra controls. You can set disable-model-invocation to true so only you can run a skill — perfect for a deploy or commit command you don't want triggered automatically. You can set user-invocable to false for background knowledge only Claude should reach for. Allowed-tools pre-approves specific tools, and context fork runs the skill in an isolated subagent. There's even dynamic injection, where a shell command runs before Claude sees the skill.

visual.alt: Claude Code frontmatter extensions like disable-model-invocation, allowed-tools, and context fork

beats.narration: Now, packaging. In Claude Code you have a choice: a standalone skill in a dot-claude directory, or a plugin. Standalone is right for personal, project-specific work and quick experiments, and gives you a short name like slash hello. A plugin is right when you want to share with a team or community, reuse across projects, and ship versioned releases. The tradeoff is that plugin skills are namespaced, like slash my-plugin colon hello, which is exactly what prevents naming conflicts.

visual.alt: Standalone versus plugin, with short names versus namespaced names

beats.narration: A plugin is a directory with a manifest at dot-claude-plugin slash plugin.json, declaring a name — which becomes the skill namespace — plus a description and an optional version. The components live at the plugin root, not inside dot-claude-plugin: a skills folder, agents, hooks, a dot-mcp.json for MCP servers, even LSP servers. That last point is the common mistake — only plugin.json goes inside the dot-claude-plugin folder; everything else sits at the root.

visual.alt: A plugin directory tree next to a minimal plugin.json manifest

beats.narration: To share a plugin, you distribute it through a marketplace — a repository with a marketplace.json that others add to Claude Code. You test locally with claude plugin-dir pointing at your folder, and run claude plugin validate before submitting. Anthropic runs two public marketplaces: a curated official one, and a community one where third-party submissions land after review, pinned to a specific commit. For team-only distribution, you just host the marketplace in a private repository.

visual.alt: Local testing commands and the two Anthropic marketplaces

beats.prompt: Why are a plugin's skills namespaced as plugin-name:skill-name?

options.text: To prevent naming conflicts between plugins
Because plugins cannot contain more than one skill
Because standalone skills are entirely forbidden

beats.correct_feedback: Right. Namespacing keeps same-named skills from different plugins from colliding.
beats.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Plugin skills are namespaced as plugin-name colon skill-name to prevent naming conflicts between different plugins.
